news.title: Labor splurge adds to $200b debt pile, drags out precarious recovery
news.snippet: Victorian Treasurer Jaclyn Symes unveils a big-spending pre-election budget to shore up the government's political future, even as debt mounts further.

Ratings agency S&P has warned that Victoria’s precarious fiscal recovery has been prolonged by Labor’s decision to spend any budget revenue gains rather than reduce debt, which is now forecast to hit nearly $200 billion after an $18 billion pre-election spending spree.

Set against a backdrop of rising debt, heightened global uncertainty, higher inflation and increasing interest rates, Treasurer Jaclyn Symes unveiled another big-spending pre-election budget on Tuesday that sought to shore up Labor’s chances of winning a historic fourth term on November 28.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.236%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/ce8c192bc47c5dea6a96964ff0ef7c50ce504cf5fb0702c8dbdfacd0b22c867e)

Premier Jacinta Allan and Treasurer Jaclyn Symes handed down Victoria’s pre-election budget on Tuesday. Bethany Rae

The Allan government is banking on strong economic growth to manage the state’s finances, but S&P warned the rosy outlook in the budget papers risked undermining its fiscal forecasts and capacity to drive down net debt as a share of the economy as promised.

The $18 billion of new spending since a mid-year budget update in December comprised the budget centrepiece of $2.5 billion in cost-of-living relief and $4.5 billion for infrastructure projects. That was offset by $607.5 million in savings on top of the $4 billion announced in response to last year’s review of the public service.

That cash splash came as the budget papers showed the state’s net debt was on track to climb to $199.3 billion in mid-2030 and gross debt to soar to $244 billion.

Advertisement

Interest repayments will lift from $8.9 billion next financial year – 7.8 per cent of government revenue – to $11.8 billion, or 9.56 per cent of revenue, at the end of the decade.

Symes tipped an operating surplus of $727 million this financial year – $17 million more than forecast in December – despite receiving an extra $5 billion in revenue in 2025-26. Operating surpluses over the next three years were $1 billion lower than projected in December.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

However, once the cost of capital infrastructure projects was considered, Victoria’s budget bottom line continued to deteriorate. The state was forecast to have a $7.7 billion cash deficit next financial year, $400 million more than projected in December.

Symes’ operating surplus was propped up by $3 billion in federal road and rail funding as well as a $1.15 billion payment from the Lottery Corporation, which secretly negotiated a 40-year extension to its exclusive licence in Victoria. If federal funding had remained at 2024-25 levels of $1.5 billion and the state did not secure the Lottery Corporation payment, Victoria would have remained in an operating deficit this year and next.

Advertisement

“This budget is about … helping with the cost of living, it is about giving families more time in their day, it is about strengthening the systems Victorians are relying on more than ever before,” Symes said in her budget speech to parliament.

“It is about building a stronger economy, a more secure future and opportunity for every Victorian. It doesn’t solve every issue that families are facing or stop the wars and shocks and impacts from overseas. But at its core it helps to restore that basic deal that if people work hard, they deserve a decent life.”

The $18 billion in new spending over the next four years was down from $27 billion last year, which Symes said demonstrated fiscal constraint. It factored in $5 billion for contingencies, which could include an election war chest. The centrepiece announcement was a $2.5 billion cost-of-living package to deal with rising inflation and soaring fuel prices due to the Middle East war.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

“Victoria’s fiscal performance is gradually improving from protracted deficits and rapidly increasing debt,” S&P analyst Rebecca Hrvatin said. “The Australian state’s budget outlines the lengthy fiscal recovery following the pandemic. The recovery had been drawn out because of decisions to spend most of its positive revenue surprises over recent years.”

Labor’s fiscal strategy was predicated on a rosy economic outlook. Treasury forecast Victoria’s economy would grow 1.75 per cent this financial year and 1.5 per cent next year, reaching 2.5 per cent at the end of the forward estimates. The state’s net debt levels as a proportion of gross state product would slowly decline from 24.9 per cent next financial year to 24.4 per cent in mid-2040.

Advertisement

Westpac senior economist Pat Bustamante said the forecasts were far more resilient than the bank’s and would weigh on the state’s fiscal position. Westpac forecast Victoria’s economy to grow 0.8 per cent in 2026-27.

Hrvatin also said Victoria’s economic assumptions had “downside risks”.

“For instance, Victoria’s oil and gas price forecasts are lower than ours and they assume a faster normalisation following the war in the Middle East. A more prolonged disruption could undermine the government’s fiscal forecasts through higher interest rates, lower consumption, and higher unemployment.”

Symes tabled her budget an hour before the Reserve Bank announced it would lift the cash rate by 0.25 of a percentage point to 4.35 per cent. Following the decision, RBA governor Michele Bullock said state and federal governments making up “the shortfalls for households by giving them more money” made it harder to dampen demand. Federal Treasurer Jim Chalmers said state government spending “mattered” in contributing to inflation.

Symes disputed her debt strategy relied on a rosy economic forecast, saying the Victorian economy was on a “firm footing” even as it entered a period of high uncertainty due to national and global challenges.

The government will rake in $115.6 billion in the next financial year and outlay $114.5 billion on operating expenses.

Advertisement

But it would take a hit in 2027 from a slump in the property market, with stamp duty revenue forecast to fall to $10 billion next year from $10.6 billion previously forecasts. Taxes on employers would rise from $12.54 billion next year to $14.52 billion within four years.

Employee expenses are expected to rise from $40.2 billion in 2025-26 to $45.5 billion in 2029-30.

The budget recovery also hinges on capital spending falling from $23.5 billion this financial year to $15.3 billion in 2029-30. S&P said it expected infrastructure spending to remain elevated at more than $20 billion per year.

Opposition Leader Jess Wilson said the budget demonstrated the government was not serious about taking responsibility for rising debt.

“Victorians now will be paying an interest bill next year that could fund police, ambos and kindergarten and leave $1 billion left in change,” Wilson said.

Business Council of Australia chief executive Bran Black said: “Businesses were looking for a credible plan to reduce debt, ease the tax burden and cut red tape. This budget doesn’t deliver that, and, until it does, Victoria will struggle to reverse its last-place ranking on business competitiveness.”

Fitch Ratings senior director Paul Norris said the budget’s revenue was boosted by a larger GST pool and additional federal funding, but that it appeared the gains were “offset by further spending commitments, including higher public sector wage payments”.

GameStop is trying to buy eBay for about $US56 billion ($78 billion) in cash and stock, a bold attempt by chief executive Ryan Cohen to take over a storied e-commerce name several times larger.

The gaming retail chain has offered $US125 per share in cash and stock for the online marketplace, or about a 20 per cent premium to its Friday close.

To steal a phrase from Fremantle Football Club’s [rival](https://www.afr.com/companies/sport/inside-the-demise-of-one-of-australia-s-richest-sports-clubs-20250822-p5mp39) West Coast’s theme song, the Dockers are flying high. Seven on the trot, second on the AFL ladder, and a line-up dominating across the ground – could this finally be its premiership year?

AFL CEO **Andrew Dillon** and his troops at [AFL House](https://www.afr.com/companies/sport/melbourne-demons-sack-ceo-paul-guerra-despite-team-s-stellar-run-20260428-p5zrsq) seem to think so. The league is trying to trademark “WHARFIE TIME”, no doubt thinking ahead to the lucrative merchandise and marketing potential of the Dockers’ inaugural flag.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.252%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/f2cf77136f97494f5899e2171dfeee40c5a30b43)

Matthew Pavlich back when he was on Wharfie Time. Getty

The phrase was first associated with Freo back in the mid-1990s when, as the league’s newest club, it frequently copped the Sunday afternoon time slot for home games. Commentators started saying this was “wharfie time”, playing up Fremantle’s blue-collar roots to suggest they were clocking on for work as everyone else relaxed.

But it was **Matthew Pavlich**, Freo’s most beloved son, who made the phrase famous in his widely-mocked captain’s speech to players in 2009. “Put on your Superman capes, boys, it’s Wharfie Time!” he implored.

It’s a hard call on what’s the greater irony – the AFL trying to reconstruct a working-class narrative for Fremantle, which has become the hipster capital of the west, or attempting to monetise Pavlich’s phrase ahead of finals. He is now chief executive of the Sydney Swans, who are top of the ladder and looking like Freo’s biggest September foe.

Advertisement

No wonder the AFL’s administration wants to focus on finals, though, given the off-field issues it’s faced lately. There are criticisms around its policing of players’ behaviour after umpire abuse and [homophobia](https://www.afr.com/companies/sport/workers-are-sacked-for-homophobia-at-work-so-should-afl-players-20250822-p5mp0g) scandals. Its overhaul of the national draft system, released last week, has also got some clubs offside. Then Carlton’s management of **Elijah Hollands’** mid-match mental health episode in April sparked concerns about players’ wellbeing, which are ongoing (the AFL [fined the Blues](https://www.theage.com.au/sport/afl/carlton-to-be-fined-75-000-for-hollands-handling-20260505-p5ztzo.html) $75,000 over this on Tuesday).

There’s no shortage of corporate high-flyers who enjoy a bit of wharfie time. BHP rising star **Jessica Farrell** is on Freo’s board and travelled to Melbourne for last Friday’s win over the Western Bulldogs.

Then there’s South32 CEO **Graham Kerr**, who is also a Dockers director and who is still running the mining company more than 12 months after it announced his departure. As one West Australian quipped, South32’s board looks to be getting inspiration from the AFL’s approach to succession planning under fellow sandgroper **Richard Goyder**– the one that saw **Gil McLachlan**’s [farewell tour](https://www.afr.com/companies/games-and-wagering/gillon-mclachlan-is-betting-on-himself-20240926-p5kdr8) extended for over a year.

The working theory within the mining industry on why Kerr is stalling (his successor **Matt Daley** was locked in a year ago) is because he’s trying to close an acquisition before he leaves, perhaps of the West Musgrave copper and nickel mine. Which, incidentally, Farrell used to run in her previous job as boss of BHP’s Nickel West. WA’s a small place!

The AFL’s Wharfie Time trademark application is unusual, as it typically sticks to claiming phrases associated with league-wide initiatives (“ [Gather Round](https://www.afr.com/rear-window/anika-wells-exile-ends-at-afl-s-top-table-20260409-p5zmhg)”) or straight team names (“Tasmanian Devils”). But its marketing pros must see some opportunity in a Freo premiership that isn’t there for other clubs, given it’s made just one similarly niche application in the past five years.

That was for “FLAGMANTLE” in 2024. Hardly reassuring for Dockers fans, given their cape-wearing wharfie boys went from third on the ladder with four rounds to go that season to not even making finals.

Sydney, meanwhile, did make it before getting hammered in the grand final.

_Yerevan, Armenia_ \| European leaders said on Monday (Tuesday AEST) that President Donald Trump’s surprise decision to pull thousands of US troops out of Germany was just the latest signal that Europe must take more responsibility for its security.

Last week the Pentagon announced that it would withdraw about 5000 troops from Germany, but Trump told reporters on Saturday the US planned on “cutting a lot further”.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.094%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/4cbd8aab05810a1ac2a50d672cb59b90d59bd928)

European Union foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas arrives for the EU-Armenia summit at the Presidential Palace in Yerevan, Armenia, on Tuesday.  AP

Trump offered no reason for the move, which blindsided NATO. But his decision came amid an escalating dispute with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, who said the US had been humiliated by Iran in talks to end the war it launched with Israel on February 28.

Trump has also expressed anger over European allies’ reluctance to get involved in the conflict.

European leaders meeting at a summit in Yerevan, Armenia, sought to downplay the impact of 5000 fewer troops in Germany while acknowledging that it provided a useful nudge for the continent to step up its role within NATO.

Advertisement

“I do not see those figures as dramatic, but I think they should be handled in a harmonious way inside the framework of NATO,” Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Store said.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said “there needs to be a stronger European element in NATO, I have no doubt about that”.

Tensions within NATO have mounted since the second Trump administration came into office last year warning that European allies would have to defend themselves and Ukraine in the future. Talks on ending the war there, now in its fourth year, have bogged down as the US focuses on Iran.

## Taken by surprise

European Union foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said the timing of Trump’s announcement came as a surprise, even though there had been “talk about withdrawal of US troops for a long time from Europe”.

Asked whether she believes Trump is trying to punish Merz, Kallas said: “I don’t see into the head of President Trump, so he has to explain it himself.”

Advertisement

[Merz](https://www.afr.com/world/europe/merz-claims-win-for-german-conservatives-far-right-also-makes-gains-20250224-p5lei8) did not attend the European Political Community summit in Yerevan, which included about 30 European leaders, plus Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney.

At a military exercise in northern Germany, the country’s defence minister, Boris Pistorius, said Berlin had not yet received “official confirmation of when and how this is supposed to happen, on what scale”.

The reduction of US troops “would not put into question NATO’s deterrence capability”, he added.

European countries and Canada have increased defence spending and military recruitment efforts over the past year in response to Trump’s threats.

## NATO seeks clarity

NATO Secretary-General [Mark Rutte](https://www.afr.com/world/europe/rubio-reassures-europe-but-warns-of-dangerous-delusion-20260214-p5o2ca) also played down the significance of fewer US troops in Germany, while acknowledging US “disappointment” about the level of European support for the Iran war.

Advertisement

France and the UK have given US forces limited use of bases on their territories to attack Iran. Spain has outright denied US forces the use of its airspace and bases.

Rutte, who has championed Trump’s leadership at NATO despite the US president’s criticism of a majority of the allies, said: “I would say the Europeans have heard a message.”

European allies and Canada have known since early last year that Trump would pull some troops out of Europe – and some were pulled out of Romania in October – but US officials had pledged to co-ordinate any moves with NATO allies to avoid creating a security vacuum.

NATO spokesperson Allison Hart said over the weekend that officials at the 32-nation military alliance “are working with the US to understand the details of their decision on force posture in Germany”.

## Iran and trade trouble

With the ceasefire between the US and Iran looking shakier, Rutte said European nations “have decided to pre-position assets, key assets, close to the theatre for the next phase”. He provided no further details.

Advertisement

European leaders have insisted their countries would not help police the Strait of Hormuz, a key energy trade route, until the war is over.

“If the United States is ready to reopen Hormuz, that’s great. That’s what we’ve been asking for since the beginning,” said French President Emmanuel Macron. But he underlined that Europeans were not ready to get involved in any operation “that does not seem clear”.

The Albanese government’s latest [mooted attack on investors and small businesses](https://www.afr.com/wealth/personal-finance/the-massive-tax-exposure-your-family-trust-may-be-missing-20260312-p5o9ww) is allegedly a Treasury proposal to introduce a minimum tax of 25 per cent to 30 per cent on trusts. That is, tax them like – or more like – companies.

The Howard government last considered such a proposal back in 1998-99 before abandoning it in the face of immense complexity around definitions, carve-outs and its deleterious impact on small businesses and farmers.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.215%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_74%2C$y_56/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/ed02bed754bde92de6332b7a193282caf3ba2970)

The Howard government opted against the proposal back in the late 1990s as it was deemed impractical. AAP

According to the [Australian Taxation Office](https://www.afr.com/wealth/tax/worried-by-the-ato-s-new-trust-crackdown-here-s-what-you-can-do-20251126-p5niny), in 2022-23 there were 1.02 million people who generated almost $500 billion of business income that year and held almost $3 trillion of assets. Some 250,000 had net small business income. If superannuation funds are included, and they are also a form of trust, then the total number is more than 1.5 million such entities.

Trusts are a very common business and investment vehicle in Australia and vary from small, single-asset holding ones to vast managed investment ones with billions of dollars under management.

They are used for a multiplicity of purposes, from small family trusts to very large trusts, such as unit and fixed managed ones, that allow for sharing the investment returns across a larger investor base. There are also a range of trusts that hold assets for deceased assets (testamentary trusts), disabled or incapacitated beneficiaries and charitable purposes.

Advertisement

They are used for asset protection purposes and are a more flexible and lower-cost option than a company. For example, a trust does not need to pay the annual ASIC fee of almost $400 applicable to small companies.

Trusts are also often paired with other legal forms when used in a business environment. For example, a start-up, small business or farmer will have investment assets, such as the shop, factory, farm or intellectual property, held in the trust and then carry on business operations as a sole trader, partnership or indeed company separate from the asset holdings.

> “If the government proposes to introduce a new ‘minimum’ tax on trusts then there are many issues to sort through, such as who will pay it.”

This helps them spread their risk and protect their investments. It also provides flexibility so that the operators of the assets can change, between generations or external parties, without also transferring the ownership of the assets.

While it is true that trusts allow the pass-through of income to beneficiaries, it is a fallacy that they do not pay. If a trust does not distribute – or pass through – its income to the beneficiaries, then it is subject to tax at the top marginal tax rate of 47 per cent (including the Medicare levy). Unlike sole traders or partnerships, but similar to companies, trusts cannot pass through losses.

This means that trusts are taxed like individuals, sole traders or partnerships except losses, which must be quarantined in the trust. If they distribute to a beneficiary who is a company, charity or superannuation fund, then their income is taxed like a company, charity or superannuation fund – that, the beneficiary’s tax status determines the taxation of their income.

Advertisement

If the government proposes to introduce a new “minimum” tax on trusts – and in this case, they presumably mean income earned by the trust on behalf of the beneficiaries – then there are many issues to sort through, such as who will pay it.

Will it be the executor/manager of the trust or the beneficiaries? When will it be paid; at the time of distribution or when the recipient has tax owing? On what type of income will it be paid; business income, capital gains, rents, royalties, unrealised gains, interest payments on loans?

Will this new trust tax apply instead of the current (top personal marginal rate) tax applicable to trusts on undistributed income? Will the new trust be a final withholding tax; that is, once paid, no further tax is payable, in which investors, including foreign ones – where this would be a breach of double tax treaties – or superannuation funds – with their lower tax rates – would pull their investments?

If the new trust tax is not a final withholding tax, but rather a creditable tax (whether withheld and paid upfront by the trust or not), akin to a franking credit, will such tax be refundable in the hands of the ultimate beneficiary?

If it isn’t, then taxpayers on lower rates – whether individuals, charities or various investors like superannuation funds – will effectively be overtaxed on trust income, while those on higher rates are either no worse off or better off.

And if the akin to imputation/franking route is pursued, does that mean the more than 1 million trusts will now be subject to a whole new set of complex and expensive to manage franking account type rules? Noting that they can’t be the same as for companies as the two entities are fundamentally different legal structures.

Advertisement

Finally, that leaves two very big issues to also sort through before making any announcement.

One, will such a new trust tax regime be designed to eliminate the deliberate policy tax preferences that flow with income from trusts? That includes depreciation and capital allowances; capital gains discounts; and specific concessions ranging from R&D and early-stage investing to landcare and carbon abatement. In which case, those deliberate policy measures will be wiped out in one fell swoop.

Two, the form of grandfathering and restructuring or rollover relief that will be made available must be clarified.

In working through these issues, it was clear when the Howard government considered such a measure that it would be very messy, complicated and, at the end of the day, counterproductive with vast resources wasted in working through its implications.

Those include the potentially significant movement and restructure of assets; grandfathering and cross-over implementation provisions; and the multitude of definitions of different trusts that were in or out of the regime.

At the end of the day, what would this nightmare all be for? $100 million or $200 million of possible earlier collected taxation at a cost of vast disruption to up to $500 billion of business income and $3 trillion of investment.

Australia is [one of the most privately wealthy nations in the world](https://www.afr.com/wealth/personal-finance/great-wealth-transfer-20260302-p5o6mo).

Outside superannuation and housing, a substantial share of national wealth is held in privately owned and family-controlled businesses – property groups, agribusinesses, industrial firms, professional practices and founder-led enterprises that employ millions of Australians and underpin entire regional economies.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.738%2C$multiply_2%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/002107e1a30c0779023b4d44bb666fed627579fa)

The hit series Succession explores the family issues that can arise when a business passes from one generation to the next.

Yet despite this scale, sophistication and size of wealth, succession remains the most persistent and destructive vulnerability in Australia’s private wealth system. It is not a minor issue, nor one limited to ageing founders. It is a structural weakness that continues to erode family capital, fracture relationships and destroy otherwise durable businesses.

Succession is still seen as a personal transition issue, even though it is really about capital and control. This is evidenced by the data, which is quite confronting.

Fewer than 30 per cent of family businesses survive into the second generation and fewer than 15 per cent reach the third. These results are not driven by poor assets or weak markets. Nor did the business fail. Often, these businesses were financially strong when succession began.

Advertisement

Overwhelmingly, succession failures arise from governance issues, misaligned incentives and succession planning that is either deferred, avoided or fundamentally misunderstood.

In other words, they are consistently human and structural problems, not strategic ones. The tragedy is that they are predictable – and therefore preventable.

How can the chances of succession failure be reduced? A good first step is to understand the main traps that lead to failures. Here are six to consider.

## 1\. Treating succession as a technical exercise

Most succession planning starts with the wrong questions. Tax. Valuation. Structure. Those issues matter, but they are secondary. A family business operates across two systems: one commercial and rational, the other emotional, based on identity and lifelong ties. In family enterprises, these systems collide.

When owners avoid answering personal questions, such as: when do I step back? How much capital do I need outside the business? Who am I if I’m no longer running this? Succession stalls. Control is retained. Wealth remains illiquid. Decision-making is quietly paralysed.

Advertisement

## 2\. Avoiding the hard conversations

Many founders pride themselves on their resilience and self-reliance. However, that strength can often become a liability. Business owners frequently delay asking for help because it feels like a loss of control. In family businesses, that reluctance is magnified by emotion and history.

The result is predictable. Succession discussions are postponed until illness, fatigue or conflict forces decisions under pressure – when wealth destruction accelerates.

## 3\. Assuming wealth produces capable successors

Inherited capital does not equal leadership capability. While many next-generation family members seek greater responsibility, far fewer feel confident in making major decisions.

History offers harsh lessons from businesses that collapsed, not because opportunity disappeared, but because capability was missing at the critical moment.

Advertisement

## 4\. Managing large private wealth with informal governance

Many Australian families control substantial private capital with minimal formal oversight. This approach works – until it doesn’t. Weak governance magnifies conflict and erodes value during succession. Family relationships become politicised, decision-making slows and capital declines.

## 5\. Treating succession as a transaction

Succession is often framed as a transaction with a handover date. In reality, it is a long, uneven process influenced by health, markets, capability and family dynamics. Treating it as a one-off event leaves families vulnerable when circumstances change.

## 6\. Assuming succession must mean family operation

This is the most expensive assumption of all. Much succession planning assumes that the business must remain family operated. The data suggests otherwise. Often, the next generation does not want – or is not suited – to manage the core business, even though they want to retain ownership of the capital it generates. Treating this as a failure is a profound error.

Advertisement

## The solutions

How can these traps for succession failure be overcome? Here are some steps to consider.

**Start with the owner, not the structure**

Succession must begin with owner-first planning. That means clarity on personal financial independence, timing, post-transition role and legacy objectives.

Until the owner is financially and emotionally prepared to step back, business succession is theoretical at best.

When personal clarity exists, structural decisions become executable rather than symbolic.

Advertisement

**Institutionalise the conversation**

Successful families do not rely on informal discussions. They establish structured forums – family councils, facilitated planning sessions or governance reviews – where difficult issues are anticipated and addressed early.

Process matters. When families agree on what the issues are before debating solutions, emotion gives way to clarity and progress becomes possible.

**Define readiness and separate ownership from management**

Leadership readiness must be explicit, not assumed. External experience, structured development, rotation through key roles and exposure to real decision-making are essential.

Equally important is separating ownership from management. [Not every family member should run the business](https://www.afr.com/wealth/personal-finance/do-these-4-things-if-your-kids-can-t-or-won-t-take-over-the-business-20250501-p5lvn9). When this distinction is clear, professionalism increases and resentment decreases.

Advertisement

**Professionalise structure and governance early**

Governance should protect both capital and relationships. Independent directors, clear decision rights and formal rules concerning family employment help to reduce conflict and improve decision-making.

Good governance does not constrain families. It preserves them, especially when pressure rises.

**Treat succession as a discipline, not an event**

The most successful transitions are staged, deliberate and adaptive. Families that revisit succession regularly – reassessing readiness, governance and timing – preserve optionality and value.

Ego, not structure, is usually the obstacle.

Advertisement

**Separate control from capital**

For many private enterprises, the optimal succession outcome is not intergenerational management but a smooth transition from an operating business to a capital or investment structure, typically through the sale of the business.

Professional management, diversified investment portfolios and a shift from operators to stewards often preserve far more wealth – and harmony – than forced family control.

Succession success should be measured not by who runs the business, but by whether family wealth is protected, governed and grown.

**The real risk – and the real opportunity**

The greatest risk to Australia’s private and family wealth is not volatility or regulation. It is delayed action – and outdated assumptions.

Advertisement

Succession may have been scripted drama, but its central lesson is real. When founders avoid decisions, family dynamics eventually force them, often at the worst possible moment.

Succession is not about letting go. It is about stewardship – of capital, relationships and legacy.

The strongest families are not those who cling to control the longest, but those who recognise when letting go is the most disciplined act of stewardship they can make.
